coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of tin oxide @ graphitic carbon nitride quantum dot nanocomposite as an electrode material for supercapacitor application

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles enticed recent researchers with their remarkable application potential as a high-performance supercapacitor device. In this paper, on account of its low cost and non-toxicity of SnO2 metal oxide, it is embedded with graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) for supercapacitor application. One pot hydrothermal method is utilized to synthesize SnO2/CNQDs nanocomposite (Sn-CN NC) materials. The structural and constituents of chemical present in the synthesized samples were studied by XRD, FTIR and Raman. In XRD, the tetragonal rutile structure of SnO2 is compatible with Sn-CN NC without any additional peaks. The presence of functional groups was confirmed by the FTIR spectrum and the presence of D and G bands confirms the presence of CNQDs in Sn-CN NC in the Raman spectrum. The chemical composition and oxidation state were further analyzed by the XPS spectrum. The sphere-like morphology was observed in FESEM and HR-TEM. The HR-TEM further confirms the crystallinity of the Sn-CN NC by the SAED pattern. The weight percentage of Sn-CN NC was observed from the EDS spectrum. The electrochemical analysis of the prepared nanocomposite was investigated using CV, GCD and EIS spectrum. The Sn-CN NC delivers a high specific capacity of 314.8C/g at a current density of 1 A/g. The Sn-CN NC//AC device shows maximal energy and power density of about 72.32 Wh/Kg and 5624 W/Kg. The fabricated device has good retention of cyclic stability and coulombic efficiency. For good electrochemical performance of Sn-CN NC, this optimized NC electrode is a potential candidate in energy storage applications.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of tin oxide @ graphitic carbon nitride quantum dot nanocomposite as an electrode material for supercapacitor application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles enticed recent researchers with their remarkable application potential as a high-performance supercapacitor device. In this paper, on account of its low cost and non-toxicity of SnO2 metal oxide, it is embedded with graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) for supercapacitor application. One pot hydrothermal method is utilized to synthesize SnO2/CNQDs nanocomposite (Sn-CN NC) materials. The structural and constituents of chemical present in the synthesized samples were studied by XRD, FTIR and Raman. In XRD, the tetragonal rutile structure of SnO2 is compatible with Sn-CN NC without any additional peaks. The presence of functional groups was confirmed by the FTIR spectrum and the presence of D and G bands confirms the presence of CNQDs in Sn-CN NC in the Raman spectrum. The chemical composition and oxidation state were further analyzed by the XPS spectrum. The sphere-like morphology was observed in FESEM and HR-TEM. The HR-TEM further confirms the crystallinity of the Sn-CN NC by the SAED pattern. The weight percentage of Sn-CN NC was observed from the EDS spectrum. The electrochemical analysis of the prepared nanocomposite was investigated using CV, GCD and EIS spectrum. The Sn-CN NC delivers a high specific capacity of 314.8C/g at a current density of 1 A/g. The Sn-CN NC//AC device shows maximal energy and power density of about 72.32 Wh/Kg and 5624 W/Kg. The fabricated device has good retention of cyclic stability and coulombic efficiency. For good electrochemical performance of Sn-CN NC, this optimized NC electrode is a potential candidate in energy storage applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The global production of electricity mostly relies on energy obtained from fossil fuels such as natural gas, coal and oil. It is surmised that these resources may be diminished within the next century. Due to the high rate of energy consumption, it is essential to store and use energy, effective storage methods include pumped hydroelectric, compressed air, flywheels in energy storage devices, etc. Supercapacitors, as energy storage systems with rapid discharge capabilities, are well suited for applications that demand high power within a short amount of time [1–3]. Nevertheless, a significant challenge in current research is the effective and efficient utilization of the energy and power stored in systems. Among energy storage devices, supercapacitors stand out as the most promising. In the past decade, extensive research has been undertaken to enhance energy storage capabilities and expand the applications of supercapacitors [4]. Supercapacitors an energy storage devices characterized by low maintenance cost, longevity, exorbitant power density and rapid charging abilities. However, rival to secondary batteries, supercapacitors energy density is lower. To develop supercapacitors with enhanced energy and power density, as well as longer life cycles, enhanced electrode materials are required [5]. Like batteries, supercapacitors comprise compounds such as two electrodes, electrolytes and a separator.

The efficacy of supercapacitors greatly relies on the characteristics of their electrode materials. Based on the types of electrodes and charge storage mechanisms, supercapacitors are categorized into three types: Electrochemical double-layer capacitors (EDLC), pseudocapacitors and hybrid supercapacitors. At the electrode-electrolyte interface, EDLCs accumulate energy electrostatically via the absorption and desorption of electrolyte ions [6,7]. In a double layer, the static distance that separates the charges remains within the nanometer range. In addition, EDLC which known for maintaining a specific surface area that does not undergo any redox reactions due to the usage of electrochemically stable electrodes. Carbon and carbon derivatives are explored and utilized as electrode materials in EDLC. At the electrode/electrolyte interface, the charge transfer occurs through which pseudocapacitors store charge through a fast and reversible faradaic reaction. Compared to EDLC, pseudocapacitors possess unparalleled energy densities and specific capacitance owing to the faradaic process involved [7–9]. Materials like transition metal oxides and conducting polymers are the preferred electrodes for pseudocapacitors. The hybrid supercapacitor (HSC), a combined feature of EDLC and pseudocapacitor, has an elevated characteristic compared to individual supercapacitors [10]. In comparison to EDLC, HSCs have a higher energy and power density than batteries respectively. This feature ought to allow HSC to bridge the gap in the Ragone graph between EDLC and batteries. By using the appropriate type of electrode and electrolyte materials, the performance of HSC was enhanced. Depending on the type of electrode configuration, HSC can be either asymmetric, composite or battery-type. Asymmetric hybrid capacitors integrate faradaic and non-faradaic processes through the coupling of an EDLC with the pseudocapacitor electrodes. Conducting polymers and activated carbon are quintessential examples of asymmetric hybrid supercapacitors [11,12]. This kind of capacitor offers superior energy and power density than EDLCs as well as more desirable cyclic stability. Further, through faradaic reactions, pseudocapacitor materials are capable of elevating the capacitance of the composite electrode. These composites exhibit a prolonged cycling stability in comparison to EDLC.

Battery-type hybrid supercapacitors are comprised of two electrodes: a battery electrode and a supercapacitor (SC) electrode. This type of SC comprises the characteristics of both SC and battery within a single cell. This synergistic design of SC integrates the energy features of batteries with the power, cycle life and recharge durations of supercapacitors to fulfil the requirements for more energy supercapacitors and high-power batteries [13–15]. The electrode material, serving as an active region where charge is stored, is an especially crucial component of a supercapacitor. Metal oxide often possesses significantly higher specific capacitance due to its distinct crystal structures and multiple oxidation states of metal ions, which both contribute to superior electrochemical behavior [16,17]. A wide range of metal oxides like Fe2O3, SnO2, MnO2, NiO and RuO2 have been effectively utilized in supercapacitor applications. Tin oxide (SnO2) is an n-type semiconducting material with exceptional chemical traits that facilitate the fabrication of electrodes employed in supercapacitor applications. Moreover, SnO2 nanomaterial, for its low cost and non-toxic properties, garnered significant attention in the field of supercapacitors [18,19]. SnO2 has low electrical conductivity and limited electrolyte ion movement inside its matrix, despite if it is responded significantly to a specific capacitance of supercapacitors, its practical application remains limited. One viable way to accomplish these limitations is to combine SnO2 with other highly conductive carbonaceous materials like carbon nanotubes, graphene or activated carbon [20]. Hybrid materials comprising metal oxide with carbonaceous materials were observed to perform better in SC applications due to the synergistic effect of metal oxide pseudocapacitive qualities and double layer capacitance of carbon matrix [21].

2D materials like graphene and fullerenes are expensive as they require intricate growth control. In contrast, graphitic carbon nitride (gC3N4) is an eco-friendly, abundant, easily synthesized and metal-free material with a polymeric 2D structure. Graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs), a 0D material with size confinement that can be used as a supercapacitor electrode [22]. Additionally, CNQDs possess a large surface area and easily accessible edges that can serve as an active site for electrochemical reactions, which would potentially elevate the electrochemical behavior [23]. Prior studies have documented that the combination of SnO2 and carbon-based materials enhances electrochemical behavior. Vinoth et al. prepared SnO2 nanoparticles decorated with Multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) and Vulcon carbon (VC), synthesized through a sonochemical method. SnO2 /MWCNT delivers a higher specific capacitance than SnO2/VC, i.e., 133.33 F/g at 0.5 mA/cm−2 in 1 M Na2SO4 [24]. Xu et al. synthesized SnO2/gC3N4 by a hydrothermal method, which delivers a specific capacitance of about 488 F/g with a current density of 1 A/g. SnO2/gC3N4 has a capacitive retention of 97.2 % at a current density of 10 A/g and 73.4 % at a current density of 20 A/g. The energy density and power density of SnO2/gC3N4 were 2.8 Wh/Kg and 5684 W/kg were obtained [25]. Liu et al. prepared SnO2 nanorods/reduced graphene oxide(rGO) by a simple hydrothermal method, with a current density of 100 mA/g in 1 M Na2SO4 delivering a specific capacitance of 184.6 F/g. SnO2/rGO exhibit excellent cyclic stability of about 98 % after 6000 cycles at a sweep rate of about 50 mV/s [26]. Sepra et al. prepared SnO2 nanospheres through the hydrothermal method. Over the surface of reduced graphene oxide (rGO), the SnO2 nanospheres were electrostatically self-assembled. With an electrolyte of 1 M H2SO4, this composite delivers a specific capacitance of 337.52 F/g at 0.5 A/g. After 5000 cycles the retention was about 89 % [27]. Rani et al. synthesized mesoporous SnO2@C via the impregnation method, exhibiting a specific capacitance of about 432 F/g at 1 A/g using 1 M KOH solution as an electrolyte. For 2000 cycles, the capacitance retention was about 95.5 % [28].

In the present work, SnO2@graphitic carbon nitride quantum dot (CNQDs) was synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. Beyond its low cost and non-toxic behavior, SnO2 can be a promising material for supercapacitors. The integration of gC3N4 quantum dots (CNQDs) with metal oxides, specifically tin dioxide (SnO2), utilizing a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, represents an innovative approach in the realm of supercapacitor applications. Currently, there is limited published literature exploring the use of CNQDs in supercapacitor applications. While the synthesis of CNQDs is somewhat time-consuming, it remains a cost-effective process. Once synthesized, the incorporation of CNQDs with metal oxides through a one-pot approach simplifies the preparation methods significantly. This binary composite has the potential to exhibit favorable electrochemical properties. CNQDs possess numerous surface functional groups which easily ingress electrolyte ions and have large surface areas that facilitate the efficient transport of electrode ions. Moreover, a multitude of CNQD active sites contributes to elevated electrochemical behavior [22]. Therefore, the incorporation of CNQDs into SnO2 metal oxides provides a higher specific capacity than the SnO2 electrode material. The hybrid device SnO2@CNQDs//AC shows high energy and good capacitance retention. Consequently, this work demonstrates that SnO2@CNQDs is a promising electrode material for supercapacitor application.

Tin chloride dihydrate (SnCl2 · 2H2O) (99 %), Trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) (98 %), and Urea (CO(NH2)2) (99 %), all these compounds are used without any purification and obtained from SRL (Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., India).

CNQDs were prepared by the solid phase method, taking urea (CO(NH2)2) and Trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) in a 1:6 ratio, which were grained to make fine powder and kept in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 2 h. When the prepared sample attained room temperature, it was washed in ethanol several times to make an impurity less sample. Further, the colloidal sample was dialyzed in DI water for 24 h. After 24 h treatment, the prepared CNQDS were obtained. The synthesized CNQDS were confirmed by UV, PL and HR-TEM analysis and the respective results were reported in the supporting information.

The metal oxide Sn-CN nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal method using ethanol and DI water as a solvent, taken in a ratio of 1:1. First, 1.1 g of SnCl2.2H2O and 2.9 g of Na3C6H5O7 were added to the mixed solution and a clear solution was obtained after being stirred for 1 h then 2 mL of diluted CNQDs solution was added. The prepared solution was kept in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h. When the sample attained room temperature, it was centrifuged and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Moreover, the dried sample was annealed at 600 °C for 2 h. Finally, we obtained a Sn-CN nanocomposite powder. The SnO2 nanoparticle was prepared without the addition of CNQDs. The entire synthesized procedure is shown in Fig. 1 .

The electrochemical performance of prepared Sn-CN nanocomposites was investigated using an Origalys electrochemical workstation which was carried out with a conventional two and three-electrode system, 2 M of KOH as an electrolyte solution. Using Sn-CN NC as a working electrode, Pt as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. For the device-two electrode system, Sn-CN as a positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as a negative electrode are used. Sn-CN and SnO2 electrode was prepared by using 80 % of active material, 10 % of PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) and 10 % of AC were grained together and made into a slurry using an N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solution. Nickel foam was used as a substrate. After the basic cleaning process was carried out, the 1 × 1 cm2 substrate was coated with a prepared slurry, which was further used as a working electrode. After coating nickel foam was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. The electrochemical studies of prepared electrodes were analyzed using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge and discharge (GCD) and Electrochemical impedance (EIS).

In the three-electrode system, the specific capacity of the prepared sample was estimated from CV and GCD curves using eq. 1 and 2,

The crystalline structure and phase purity of synthesized composite materials were examined using X'Pert PRO PAnalytic powder XRD (X-ray diffractometer) in the 2θ range of 10° to 80°. The functional group of synthesized pristine SnO2 and Sn-CN nanocomposite were analyzed by Perkin Elmer, spectrum two model Fourier Transform Infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) by using KBr pellet method in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 and Raman spectra was recorded on Micro-Laser Raman (Seiki, Japan). Optical properties of CNQDs nanoparticles were identified by Thermo Fisher, Evolution 220 PC-control UV scan (UV–Vis Spectrophotometer) and Photoluminescence graph was observed by using Fluorescence Spectrophotometer- Perkin Elmer (LS 45) in the range of 200 nm to 900 nm. The morphology and molecular composition of prepared composite materials were analyzed by a Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) instrument and chemical composition by an Energy Dispersive

X-ray microanalysis (EDAX) -Thermo Fisher FEI Quanta 250 FEG. The particle size, morphology and SAED pattern of the nanocomposite were examined by FEI Teonai G2 20 S-TWIN TEM (Transmission Electron Microscope). The surface composition and oxidation state of the as-prepared composite were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Thermo Scientific, ESCALAB 250 xi.

The crystalline structure, phase and crystallinity of the as-synthesized samples SnO2 and Sn-CN nanocomposite were studied using XRD analysis (Fig. 2 ). In the XRD pattern of SnO2 and Sn-CN nanocomposite, the 2θ values of distinguished peaks are 2θ = 26.5°, 33.8°, 37.9°, 51.7°, 54.7°, 58°, 61.8°, 65.7°, and 71° corresponds to the lattice planes of (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (002), (310), (301), and (202). All the diffraction peaks are ascribed to the tetragonal structure of SnO2 (JCPDS card no: 88–0287). Sn-CN nanocomposite XRD peaks bear semblance to that of SnO2 without any additional peaks, which confirms the purity of the prepared sample. Proportionate peaks of CNQDs are not found in Sn-CN NC owing to the overlapping of the (110) peak of SnO2 [31]. Scrutinizing the peaks of Sn-CN NC, a slight shift and increase in intensity were discerned. This result shows that CNQDs are subsumed into the SnO2, which stipulates the presence of CNQDs, and that CNQDs are intercalated into the host matrix sample [32]. By utilizing this Sn-CN nanocomposite as an active electrode in a supercapacitor, we may effectively improve the electrolyte interaction, which in turn enhances capacitance and charge mobility. The average crystalline size of the synthesized samples was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer eq.
(6)
D = kλ / βcosθ
Where D is the average crystalline size in nm, k is the Scherrer constant, β is the full-width half maximum, λ represents wavelength and θ is the diffraction angle [33]. The average crystalline size of SnO2 and Sn-CN NC is 13.7 and 22.2 nm, respectively.

The purity and chemical composition of the prepared samples were investigated through an FTIR study. FTIR spectra of SnO2 and Sn-CN NC are depicted in Fig. 3a. The characteristic peak at 3433 cm−1 is ascribed to water molecules of OH stretching vibrations and the OH bending vibration peak occurs at 1639 cm−1 [34]. In Sn-CN NC, a new strong peak at 1384 cm−1 and a weak peak at 1417 cm−1 were observed, which were ascribed to stretching vibrations of CN heterocycles and the stretching vibrations of CO peak occur at 1113 cm−1 [35,36]. The corresponding peak at 770 cm−1 was ascribed to heptazine units. Further, the peak at 1339 cm−1 was ascribed to ternary (3CN) amines [37]. The vibration of the SnO bond occurs to be a prominent sharp peak at around 550–700 cm−1 [34]. From the FTIR study, we observed that CNQDs have nitrogen content groups and it is good hydrophilicity materials in nature [38]. These unique characteristics may suggest that the prepared material Sn-CN NC has superior electrochemical behavior, making it an ideal choice for use as a supercapacitor electrode material in an aqueous electrolyte.

Fig. 3b shows the Raman spectra of SnO2 and Sn-CN NC. In the SnO2 spectrum, the peak at 484, 628 and 771 cm−1 was attributed to vibration modes of Eg, A1g, and B2g. These peaks observed at pristine SnO2 are ascribed to the rutile tetragonal structure of SnO2 nanoparticles [39]. In Sn-CN NC, the extra peaks at 1336 and 1590 cm−1 correspond to carbon peaks, which further confirms the presence of CNQDs. The peaks at 1336 and 1590 cm−1 correspond to the D and G bands, which are the distinct bands of carbonaceous CNQDs nanomaterials. This signifies that the D band and G band prevail due to disordered or defect carbon and the ordered graphitic carbon of CNQDs materials [40]. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) can be evaluated to assess the degree of graphitization and structural defects [41]. The ID/IG ratio of Sn-CN NC is 1.022 higher than unity, suggesting that this increased value is due to the presence of SnO2 nanoparticles on CNQDs. This ratio manifests that the average size of Sp2 domains is diminished [26]. In the nanocomposite material, the peaks at 485, 626 and 769 cm−1 resemble those of pristine SnO2 nanoparticles. The intensity of the A1g peak of Sn-CN NC was elevated, which demonstrated a significant correlation with better crystallization and is sensitive to the degree of crystallization. This suggests that Sn-CN NC is highly crystalline in nature and that this result is compatible with the XRD and SAED pattern of Sn-CN NC. The intensity of Eg is increased in Sn-CN NC, it is increased with increasing concentration of oxygen vacancies. Substantially, the Eg is sensitive to oxygen vacancy [42]. Oxygen vacancy has a significant impact on energy storage traits by increasing carrier concentration and enhancing electrical conductivity [43].

The FESEM and HR-TEM are utilized to characterize the microstructure and panoramic morphology of the samples of SnO2 and Sn-CN nanocomposite. Fig. 4 (a,b) shows FESEM images of both SnO2 and Sn-CN NC, which are observed to be in sphere-like morphology [44]. Fig. 4(c-g) and Fig. 5 show elemental mapping and EDX spectra of Sn-CN NC. The mapping images of Sn-CN NC clearly show the presence and dispersion of Sn, C and O in the Sn-CN NC sample. The lack of additional peaks in Sn-CN NC further confirms the purity of the sample. The EDS spectrum exposed the weight percentage of Sn, C and O were observed to be in the percentage of 57.5 %, 29.3 %, 8.1 % and 5.1 % were observed, in Sn-CN NC.

Fig. 6(a) displays the HR-TEM images of Sn-CN NC. Sn-CN NC possesses a structure resembling a sphere and is composed of tiny nanoclusters interconnected in conjunction to create a continuous network [45,46]. Nanoclusters comprising granular clusters with diminished sizes offer short diffusion lengths for electrolyte ions and fast charge transfer, leading to significant capacitance even at rapid charging and discharging rates [47]. In addition, the Sn-CN nanocomposite is composed of SnO2 nanoparticles embedded in the carbon matrix. The Sn-CN NC HR-TEM image shows that both carbon and SnO2 materials are in face-to-face contact; this close contact and synergistic effect of SnO2 and CNQDs may enhance the electrochemical behavior of the prepared electrode material [25]. Fig. 6(b) illustrates the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of Sn-CN NC, which reveals that the nanocomposite possesses high crystallinity. The unique lattice structure of Sn-CN NC manifests that the structural integrity was preserved after the addition of CNQDs without any significant defects. In conjunction with the XRD results, the lattice fringes pattern with the d spacing of 3.37 nm coincides with the (101) plane of the Sn-CN NC shown in Fig. 6(c). Further d spacing of 0.23 nm corresponds to gC3N4 nanosheet. This shows that the CNQDs were successfully embedded on the surface of SnO2 nanoparticles.

The prepared composite's chemical composition and oxidation state were scrutinized through XPS analysis. The existence of CNQDs and SnO2 and their interactions in the Sn-CN nanocomposite inevitably results in particular alterations in their electronic states, which was further confirmed through XPS analysis. Fig. 7(a) illustrates the corresponding survey spectrum of Sn-CN NC. In the survey spectrum, the typical main peaks were observed at distinguished binding energies ascribed to Tin (Sn), Oxygen (O), Carbon (C) and Nitrogen (N). Fig. 7(b) depicts the Sn peaks of Sn-CN NC, the Sn peaks are deconvoluted into two peaks, Sn 3d5/2 (495.7 eV) and Sn 3d3/2 (487.2 eV), which correspond to the Sn4+ oxidation state of SnO2. The two distinct peaks with binding energy differences are around 8.5 eV, which corresponds to the binding energy of SnO2 [19]. The O 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks shown in Fig. 7(c); the peak at 531.2 eV was attributed to SnO, and the peak at 532.4 eV corresponds to OH, respectively [31]. The C 1 s and N 1 s spectra were incurred owing to the presence of CNQDs in Sn-CN nanocomposite. The Carbon C 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into two binding energy peaks at 285.5 and 289.1 eV, which were ascribed to sp2 CC and N=C-N corresponding to the heptazine unit present in CNQDs depicted in Fig. 7(d). The peak at 398.8 eV in the N 1 s spectrum [Fig. 7(e)] was ascribed to the C=N-C bond in CNQDs [36]. The observed XPS spectrum confirms the oxidation state of the prepared material and the presence of CNQDs in Sn-CN NC. Further, the observed spectrum reveals the purity of the Sn-CN NC material.

With regard to BET and BJH (Barrett Joyner Halenda) analysis, the specific surface area and pore size of the composite material Sn-CN NC are investigated. Fig. 8 (a,b) illustrates the pore size distribution and N2 absorption/desorption isotherms plot from which the surface area and pore diameter of the prepared material were identified. From the Fig. 8(a) it was demonstrated that Sn-CN NC possess type IV isotherms with a significant hysteresis loop, illustrating that the prepared material obtained a mesoporous structure. The value of P/P0 from 0.6 to 1 for Sn-CN NC indicates that the Sn-CN NC possess a mesoporous structure. The obtained specific surface area (SSA) of Sn-CN NC is 28.29 m2/g. The increased surface area of the nanocomposite material is due to the presence of CNQDs, which signifies that carbon possesses a greater surface area in contact with the electrolytes [48]. This enhancement behavior in BET is expected to improve the capacitors' ability to retain charge. The pore size distribution is the sole determinant of the SSA, the BJH method was employed to assess the pore size distribution. From Fig. 8(b), it was illustrated that the determined pore size is about 3.73 nm. The considerate SSA with an average pore diameter which facilitates an adequate number of channels for the kinetics of ion-electron transport. Furthermore, the increased surface area increases the number of active sites owing to the presence of CNQDs, which enhances the electron transport kinetics [49,50]. The combination of SnO2 with 0D CNQDs improves the surface properties of the composite Sn-CN NC, while this composite enhances the electrochemical performance of the charge transport channel and interaction sites for ions.

The three-electrode configuration with 2 M KOH as an electrolytic solution was employed to study the electrochemical performance of the prepared SnO2 and SN-CN NC electrodes using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge and discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 9 (a,b) shows the CV graphs of both SnO2 and Sn-CN NC with different scan rates of 10–200 mV/s in a potential window in the range of 0–0.6 V. Owing to its relative remarkable stability across a broad range of temperature and voltage, the KOH electrolyte is preferred [51]. In both CV graphs, the peak current density value and its location in the redox potential increased with the elevation of the scan rate, indicating rapid ion diffusion and absorption processes occurring with the electrodes [52]. The anodic peak position exhibits a shift towards a higher potential, and the cathodic peaks move towards a lower potential when the scan rate is increased. This phenomenon occurs due to the elevated charge/discharge rates associated with the higher scan rates [53]. Comparing these CV graphs, it is obvious that the Sn-CN NC electrode delivers a higher current density than the SnO2 electrode material. Fig. 9(b) shows a distinctive redox peak of Sn-CN NC CV curves, which reveals its faradaic (battery-like) activity. The specific capacity calculated from CV graphs using eq. 1 was estimated to be 286.80, 251.84, 219.07, 196.06, 187.79, 149.02C/g for SnO2 and for Sn-CN NC, the obtained specific capacities are 322.52, 237.02, 202.33, 184.54, 174.76 and 136.63C/g respectively with different scan rates from 10 to 200 mV/s. Moreover, the Sn-CN NC delivers the highest specific capacity of 322.52C/g at a current density of 10 mV/s, owing to the increased integrated area of the prepared nanocomposite electrode material when analogized to the SnO2 electrode. This rise in the specific capacity of Sn-CN NC was obtained due to the synergistic effect of both CNQDs and SnO2. Compared to conventional carbon materials, inclusion of nitrogen in CNQDs significantly enhances their electron-donating properties, thereby greatly increasing their conductivity and surface wettability. Moreover. CNQDs can be employed as a supporting material to prevent the aggregation of SnO2, which facilitates an advantageous charge transfer pathway, which improves the electrochemical performance of Sn-CN NC [25]. In section 3.4, the morphology of prepared material with diminished sizes offers short diffusion length for electrolyte ions and fast charge transfer, leading to a significant increase in specific capacity [47]. This sphere-like morphology boosts the ionic- electronic conductivities, facilitates the accessibility of OH-ion absorption and desorption and contributes to an improved specific capacity. So, the enhancement of electrochemical behavior of Sn-CN NC due to the incorporation of CNQDs, which has a greater number of active sites for electrochemical reactions in Sn-CN NC [53].

To further investigate the charge storage mechanism, the theoretical model of the Dunn and Trasatti method was employed. In an energy storage device, specific capacity is the aggregate of both diffusive and capacitive contributions, which are analyzed by applying the Dunn method on the CV graph of the device.

The proportional contribution of the faradaic and surface-controlled reactions to the charge storage process can be estimated as

For battery-like behavior, the estimated percentage of diffusive contribution at the inner surface and capacitive contribution at the outer surface electrodes SnO2 and Sn-CN NC being involved in the electrochemical reaction were found to be 66.61 %, 78.59 % and 33.38 %, 21.04 % was shown in Fig. 10(a) which illustrate that diffusive contribution is higher than capacitive contribution this indicates that the faradaic (battery-like) reaction was utilized by the electrodes to stored charge [29,54,55].

To comprehend the kinetics of ions and electron transport in the SnO2 and Sn-CN NC electrodes, an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) study was investigated. The internal resistance of the electrode and the resistance between the electrode and the electrolyte of SnO2 and Sn-CN NC was discerned through performing EIS, carried out between the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 KHz. Fig. 10(d) shows the Nyquist plot of SnO2 and Sn-CN NC, which is plotted between the imaginary part (Z῎) of impedance vs the real part (Z`). The internal resistance (Rs) and the Warburg resistance (W) are unveiled by the Nyquist plot as a short arc line in the high frequency zone and a straight line in the middle and low frequency region, respectively [56]. The reaction occurring at the electrode/electrolyte contact accounts for the depressed arc at high frequency zone and the capacitive behavior is apparent by the straight vertical 45o line at low frequency region for both SnO2 and Sn-CN NC electrodes. This straight line is designated as the Warburg region that prevails when electrolytic ions diffuse into the material. The capacitive properties of the Sn-CN NC contribute to an enhanced response in the lower frequency region of the EIS. In the high frequency region, the solution resistance (Rs) exists at the point where the curve intersects the real axis (Z`) [20,25]. The value of Rs for both SnO2 and Sn-CN NC electrode materials was observed at 0.66 and 0.74 Ω. Owing to faradic reactions, the semi-circle was observed at the high frequency zone [19]. Sn-CN NC and SnO2 exhibit a semicircle that is of smaller diameter which is indexed as charge transfer resistance (RCT); the RCT value of SnO2 and Sn-CN NC was about 0.128 and 0.102 Ω. While the composite has a lower charge transfer resistance its RCT value is lower than SnO2, signifying that NC possesses greater conductivity than SnO2 material. Here, the presence of the Warburg zone (W) is associated with the diffusion of electrolyte ions into the material. When compared to pure material, the almost straight line of the composite indicates a better ion diffusion process [25]. Further, the mesoporous structure of the composite electrode reduces the resistance of the material and facilitates improved electrolyte ion transport within the developed composite material [52]. To transform the EIS spectrum into the appropriate equivalent circuit, the overall impedance contribution was streamlined. The overall resistance of Sn-CN NC, i.e., Rs and RCT, is reduced, which can be attributable to the synergistic effect of both CNQDs and SnO2 nanoparticles. This shows that Sn-CN NC delivers an enhanced electrochemical behavior.

Fig. 10(b, c) illustrates a galvanostatic charge and discharge plot with a potential window between 0 and 0.55 V for current densities that vary from 1 to 5 A/g in the same 2 M KOH solution. The symmetrical charge and discharge curves generated with constant current demonstrated that the supercapacitor has robust reversibility. Both SnO2 and Sn-CN NC exhibited consistent asymmetric charge and discharge curves, which demonstrate a battery-like behavior. The negligible IR drop was observed in the curve, suggesting that the total resistance of the electrode is significantly low [57]. The highest specific capacities of SnO2 and Sn-CN NC were calculated to be 100 and 314.8C/g. The Sn-CN NC significantly exhibits a longer discharge time relative to the SnO2 electrode, which indicates its charge storage capacity. The Sn-CN NC reveals good charge storage and high-power density due to the absence of a pronounced IR drop. The observation revealed that the enhanced electron conductivity of the composite electrode contributed to the significant improvement in charge transfer efficiency. The substantial surface area with efficient mesoporous structure and numerous active sites due to the incorporation of CNQDs enhances the electrochemical behavior of the composite electrode [52]. In the Sn-CN NC electrode, the cyclic retention was carried out in the same 2 M KOH electrolyte at 5 A/g under the potential window of 0–0.55 V and was evaluated for 3000 cycles. The Sn-CN NC show excellent cycling stability, as demonstrated by the result obtained; the cyclic retention of about 81.9 % of the initial specific capacity was achieved after 3000 cycles shown in Fig. 10(e), a well-defined morphology of Sn-CN NC allows it to demonstrate enhanced stability over prolonged cycles. Using Eq. 2, from the continuous charge/discharge curve, the coulombic efficiency (CE) was determined.
(13)
Coulombic efficiency =
T d
T c
× 100 %

Td and Tc corresponded to charging and discharging time with the same currents. The obtained CE was >99 % after 3000 cycles at 5 A/g, confirming the stability of the prepared Sn-CN NC electrode. The uniform and homogeneous distribution of CNQDs on the surface of SnO2 enhances the cyclic stability and coulombic efficiency, in addition to ion transport and redox reaction rate [48,58].

Investigating Sn-CN composite electrode viability for practical applications, two electrode setup performances were executed. To fabricate a hybrid device, Sn-CN NC and activated carbon act as a positive electrode and negative electrode with 2 M KOH as an electrolyte solution. The range of potential window is about 0–1.5 V. The electrochemical performance of the assembled hybrid device was investigated using CV, EIS and GCD analysis. For optimal hybrid performance, both positive and negative electrodes ought to remain equal. The mass balance ratio of both positive and negative electrodes was obtained using the formula
(14)
m +
m −
=
C m +
C m −
×
∇
V +
∇
V −

Here C is the specific capacity (C/g), ∇ V is the potential window (v) of the device and m is the mass (mg) of both positive and negative electrodes. Where + and – sign denotes the positive Sn-CN NC and the negative activated carbon electrode [59]. For hybrid Sn-CN NC//AC device has a mass balance ratio of about 2.1. Fig. 11(a) illustrates the CV plot of a hybrid device with distinguished scan rates from 10 to 80 mV/s. The shape of the CV curves is quasi-rectangular. At higher scan rates, the CV plots are almost symmetrical and at lower scan rates of 25 and 10 mV/s show a pair of redox reactions, which can be attributed to redox reactions of SnO2 [60]. The specific capacity of the Sn-CN NC//AC device obtained from CV plots was 258, 195, 135 and 95C/g at a distinguished scan rate from 10 to 80 mV/s. While the scan rates are varied from 10 to 80 mV/s, the CV curves of a hybrid device show no discernible changes, indicating optimal capacitive performances within the high potential window region.

The fabricated hybrid Sn-CN NC//AC device EIS spectrum illustrated in Fig. 11(c) shows charge transfer resistance (RCT), solution resistance (RS) and Warburg resistance (W) that elucidate the kinetics of ions and electron transport in the device. EIS is carried out in the frequency range between 0.1 Hz to 100 KHz. On Nyquists plot the intercept on the real axis at high frequency region was perceived as solution resistance (RS), the low faradaic impedance (RCT) is apparent by the smaller radii of the semi-circle and the slope of the straight line related to the diffusion resistance (W) was observed in the low frequency zone [61,62]. The value of RCT and RS inferred from the EIS Nyquist plot of the device was found to be 0.5 and 0.29 Ω, which matches with the fitted equivalent circuit that comprises RCT, RS, W and CPE. Constant phase element (CPE) is attributed to capacity in an in-homogeneous electrode surface, respectively [63]. The enhanced conductivity of the fabricated device Sn-CN NC//AC was achieved by the device's lowest value RCT and RS, which demonstrate fast ion/electron kinetics and improve device-specific capacity.

Fig. 11(b) shows the GCD curves, further confirming the capacitive properties of the fabricated device Sn-CN NC//AC with a potential window range between 0 and 1.5 V at different current densities from 1 to 5 A/g. The calculated specific capacities from GCD curves were 225, 178.8, 149.94, 111.28 and 69.35C/g. From the GCD curve, we inferred that it has less IR drop and longer discharge duration, which provides the highest specific capacity of the device. The device GCD curves show that the discharge time is extremely similar to the charge time at higher current density, demonstrating the device's excellent electrochemical reversibility and as well as good columbic efficiency [63]. The device-specific energy and power density are the crucial metrices assessing its suitability for practical applications. The energy and power density were calculated using eqs. (4) and (5), the calculated values of energy and power densities were 72.32, 55.87, 46.56, 34.77, 21.67 Wh/Kg and 1157.12, 2250, 3353.80, 4500, 5624 W/Kg. The device shows minimal energy density without diminishing power density, the device may be utilized in practical applications. The Ragone plot of this device was being compared with the previously reported literature [64–67], as shown in Fig. 11(d). The cycle stability of the fabricated device Sn-CN NC//AC was inquired up to 5000 GCD cycles at a current density of 8 A/g. The capacitive retention of the device is about 96.5 %, and the coulombic efficiency of the device was obtained to be 102 %, which unveils the efficiency of the fabricated device shown in Fig. 11(e). The Sn-CN NC device was compared with previous literature summarized in Table 1 . The overall study of the electrochemical behavior of Sn-CN NC shows enhanced properties in supercapacitor application, which may be due to the incorporation of CNQDs in Sn-CN NC. Remarkably, the Sn-CN NC is a promising candidate for future energy storage applications.

Sn-CN NC was synthesized through the one-pot hydrothermal method. Due to its size confinement and number of active sites, CNQDs are embedded with SnO2 nanoparticles. The structural and chemical composition were investigated using XRD, FTIR and Raman studies. The chemical composition and oxidation state were further analyzed by the XPS spectrum. The morphology of the prepared samples was deduced from FESEM and HR-TEM images. The weight percentage of Sn-CN NC was observed from the EDS spectrum. The electrochemical behavior of the Sn-CN NC was investigated using CV, GCD and EIS. From the BET analysis, the surface area and average pore diameter were studied. The specific capacity value determined from CV and GCD is 322.52C/g at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and 314.8C/g at a current density of 1 A/g. The obtained RS and RCT values of Sn-CN NC from the EIS spectrum are about 0.5 and 0.29 Ω. The fabricated device delivers a specific capacity of 225C/g at a current density of 1 A/g. The acquired maximal energy and power density of the device are 72.32 Wh/Kg and 5624 W/Kg. Owing to the synergistic effect of both CNQDs and SnO2, Sn-CN NC shows enhanced electrochemical behavior. Consequently, the Sn-CN NC electrode material is appropriate for utilization in supercapacitor applications.

Sudhahar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology.

The original research work titled “Fabrication of Tin Oxide @ Graphitic Carbon Nitride Quantum Dot Nanocomposite as a Prominent Electrode Material for Supercapacitor Application” has been carried out for the first time to the best of our knowledge. We assure that these original research results have not been published or sent to any journal/publishers in any form.
